course_description:
This course provides an introduction to mental health research. You will be exposed to a variety of research methodologies and analysis methods. You will then develop a research proposal which requires critical engagement with hypotheses, design, methods and data analysis methods suitable for your project topic. You will gain practical experience with database searches, pragmatic project design and planning and program management, feasibility assessment and problem solving. Ethical research considerations and ethics applications will be covered in the course.

PXMH7115 is the first of two research-focused courses in the Master of Mental Health (MMH) program. This course is designed to support students in the early development of an independent research project relevant to mental health practice in psychotherapy and/or art therapy.

Students will experience the processes of refining a research topic, reviewing and synthesising literature, exploring suitable research paradigms and methodologies, and shaping a feasible research plan. This will culminate in the development of a research proposal.

Workshops, individual supervision, self-directed study and group classes will scaffold students’ learning as they move from broad topic interests toward a clearly defined research question and methodological direction. The course aims to build students’ confidence, research identity, and scholarly capability, preparing them for ongoing project development and thesis work in PXMH7116.

Note: We encourage students to undertake applied and clinical topics in mental health that are relevant to their professional interests, and that fit within the semester timeline. Any projects that will require ethics approval must first be approved by the Course Coordinator early in the semester. Students also have the option to consider projects such as systematic reviews and heuristic self-inquiries, which typically do not require full ethics approval.

course_aims: Develop capacity to design an independent project relevant to mental health.

learning_outcomes.description: Formulate a clear research topic and question relevant to mental health.
Critically review and synthesise relevant literature to justify the research question and significance.
Select and justify an appropriate research paradigm and methodological approach suitable to the topic, including ethical considerations and approval as necessary.
Engage effectively with supervision to support research planning, ethical awareness, and project development.

assessment_details.title: Research Question & Paradigm Proposal (Oral)
assessment_details.task_description: Students will deliver a brief 5-minute oral proposal outlining their intended research topic, their emerging research question(s), and the research paradigm shaping their early thinking. The proposal should clearly describe the research topic and the question the student intends to answer, highlight its relevance to mental health practice, and explain its specific application within psychotherapy and/or art therapy. An additional Q&A component will enable students to demonstrate authentic authorship and meaningful engagement with their developing research ideas. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
Please refer to the UQ policy on late submission .

assessment_details.title: Oral Defence with Q&A
assessment_details.task_description: Students will deliver a 5-minute oral defence of their proposed research project. The defence should outline the project aims, research question(s), proposed methodology, participant or data sources, and feasibility considerations. Students may choose to use PowerPoint slides to support the structure and communication of their presentation. To support their oral defence, students will submit a brief written project overview (approximately 200-250 words). This overview will summarise the study gap driving the project, the research question(s), and the proposed methodological and feasibility plan. The oral defence will include a Q&A component in which students respond to questions about their proposal. The purpose of this assessment is to verify authentic authorship, evaluate conceptual and methodological understanding, and determine readiness to progress into thesis work. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.

assessment_details.title: Research Proposal
assessment_details.task_description: The research proposal sets out the rationale for your research project and explains how it will be implemented. The proposal must demonstrate that the project is worth undertaking (i.e., has theoretical and/or practical significance) and that it can be accomplished within the timeframe available. The proposal should include a written Introduction (approximately 2,500 words) that offers a literature review, a clear statement of the research question(s), and a rationale that identifies the study gap driving the project. A brief methodological proposal section should outline the proposed design, participant or data source selection, planned procedures, ethical considerations, and a feasibility overview that includes a project timeline (Gantt chart), bringing the total submission to approximately 3,000 words. Students should consult the rubric on Learn.UQ (Blackboard) for detailed marking expectations. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Please refer to UQs AI guidelines here . Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: [AT Students only] 2 Reflective Artworks
assessment_details.task_description: You are required to submit an electronic copy of two artwork pieces along with the title, media used, dimensions, and a short statement (approx. 40 words) about each piece. These pieces should represent a reflection on the research process, made from your position as a student researcher. Note: At the end of the research courses (in semester 2), Art Therapy students will create and exhibit a minimum of six and maximum of ten art works. These 6 -10 pieces of art should link together and provide a reflection throughout the research process. You can use any materials but be cognisant of the confines of the exhibition space. Please speak to the Course Coordinator (Dani Walsh) for details. The two artworks submitted for this assessment hurdle can be included in the final 6-10 submitted for the exhibition. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Please refer to UQs AI guidelines here . assessment_details.title: Research Process and Scholarly Engagement
assessment_details.task_description: You are allocated a research supervisor for six hours of supervision per semester. To pass this assessment piece you will be required to attend a minimum of 3 supervisory meetings with your research supervisor to discuss your project. To ensure optimal research process, it is recommended that the meetings be held in a regular pattern (for example once every fortnight) rather than all at the near proposal submission period. Your supervisor will evaluate your engagement with the research process and your use of supervision throughout the course. It is designed to recognise the professional and reflective capacities that underpin high-quality research practice. Your supervisor will assess how you: Demonstrate preparedness, initiative, and professionalism in supervisory meetings Engage with feedback and integrated it meaningfully into your evolving project Show increasing reflective awareness and self-direction across the semester Maintain ethical awareness and research integrity throughout the project This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Please refer to UQs AI guidelines here . Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Research Supervisors will report on attendance and participation.

learning_activities.topic: Individual Supervision Individual supervision with the assigned supervisor. Minimum of 3 hours and a maximum of 6 hours per semester.
Group Research Supervision Zoom-based group supervision - stream specific Dates and Zoom links will be provided on Blackboard at the commencement of the semester.
